statement: V1 is scoped to the discovery-manifest-compile-serve pipeline for 3 Node.js frameworks only. All 12 vision domains are deferred to V2+.
rationale: The vision is deliberately broad but V1 must ship a complete, testable vertical slice. Express, Fastify, and NestJS cover the majority of production Node.js API frameworks. Going deep on one ecosystem beats going shallow on many.

statement: MCP serve is describe-only in V1 — no live API proxying.
rationale: Live proxy requires auth forwarding, error normalization, and reliability handling. These are significant concerns that would delay V1. Describe-only MCP still proves the pipeline end-to-end and is useful for agent discovery.

statement: Capability grouping starts as 1:1 route-to-capability mapping. Intelligent grouping deferred.
rationale: Smart capability grouping (merging related endpoints into higher-level capabilities) is the core vision differentiator but requires heuristic tuning against real codebases. Starting 1:1 lets us ship and iterate based on real feedback.

statement: No plugin API in V1. Framework support is hardcoded for Express, Fastify, NestJS.
rationale: Premature abstraction of plugin interfaces risks building the wrong API. V1 hardcodes 3 frameworks; the plugin boundary emerges from the patterns we discover during implementation.

statement: TypeScript/JavaScript ecosystem only in V1. Python, Go, Java are V2+.
rationale: Single AST parser ecosystem (ts-morph or equivalent) lets us go deep on inference quality. Multi-language support multiplies the scanning surface without adding core product value.

statement: tusq review is non-interactive in V1 — prints summary to stdout only. Users approve capabilities by editing tusq.manifest.json directly. Interactive TUI deferred to V1.1.
rationale: Previous artifacts contradicted themselves: scope item 11 said 'interactive review/approval' but behavior section said 'prints to stdout'. V1 has no TUI — keeping it simple and consistent.

statement: tusq manifest requires an explicit prior tusq scan — no implicit scan fallback in V1.
rationale: Implicit scan is hidden complexity that makes the pipeline harder to reason about. Explicit steps are clearer for V1. Users run tusq scan then tusq manifest.

reasons: PM signoff is not approved. Found "Approved: NO" in .planning/PM_SIGNOFF.md; set it to "Approved: YES".

statement: Scan data persists to .tusq/scan.json as an internal file contract between tusq scan and tusq manifest.
rationale: The spec defined an implicit dependency between scan and manifest but never specified the intermediate file. Without this, an implementer would have to invent the storage location, risking divergence from spec intent.

statement: V1 includes automatic domain grouping — capabilities are grouped by resource name or controller (e.g., /users/* routes → 'users' domain). Cross-resource merging deferred to V1.1.
rationale: VISION.md mandates 'basic skill composition: domain grouping for generated tools' in V1. Pure 1:1 with no grouping contradicted the vision. Minimal domain assignment adds organizational value without complex heuristics.

statement: All planning docs now explicitly state V1 proves discovery + manifest + MCP exposure, not full executable capability delivery. tusq serve is describe-only.
rationale: Human feedback required honest product claims. V1 does not proxy live API calls — stating this clearly prevents overpromising and aligns expectations for V1.1 proxy work.

statement: Runtime learning is documented as a deferred core pillar of the tusq.dev vision, not a normal omitted feature. It is planned for V2.
rationale: Runtime instrumentation and production-signal-driven manifest refinement are foundational to the vision (the bridge from 'good enough' to 'continuously accurate'). Treating it as a minor omission misrepresented its importance.

statement: Planning phase is complete — all 4 required artifacts exist with approved content and no outstanding contradictions. Phase transition to implementation is warranted.
rationale: PM_SIGNOFF.md shows Approved: YES. SYSTEM_SPEC.md (12115 bytes), ROADMAP.md (4989 bytes), command-surface.md (2221 bytes) all exist with substantive content. All 11 prior decisions (DEC-001 through DEC-011) are reflected in the artifacts. No new scope changes or contradictions found on this review pass.

statement: Rejected prior attempt assumptions and revalidated against current HEAD after operator baseline drift before claiming readiness.
rationale: Attempt 1 was rejected because baseline changed post-dispatch; proceeding without rebasing verification would risk false acceptance evidence.

statement: Aligned CLI behavior with planned command surface by accepting `--verbose` across commands and printing command help on invalid flags.
rationale: This removes a concrete UX/spec mismatch and hardens user-error handling with explicit, test-covered guidance.

statement: Rejected attempt-1 QA artifacts as stale and re-ran all verification from scratch against current HEAD to close the baseline drift that caused attempt-1 rejection.
rationale: The previous attempt was invalidated because an implementation checkpoint commit landed after QA was dispatched, making its evidence unreliable. Correct QA discipline requires re-running all checks against the actual HEAD rather than trusting prior evidence.

statement: Ship verdict remains SHIP. No new issues were found in the re-verification pass; the implementation matches the V1 contract (DEC-001 through DEC-011) and all 22 acceptance criteria pass.
rationale: Smoke test suite exited 0; manual CLI spot-checks for help, version, unknown command, invalid flag, and per-command --verbose all behaved as specified. The implementation changes introduced in the dev checkpoint (--verbose flag acceptance and command usage on invalid flags) are correct and covered by the test suite.

statement: Rejected the assumption implied by the prior QA ship verdict that launch readiness followed automatically; the required launch artifacts were still placeholders and public-facing entry points overstated V1.
rationale: QA validated product behavior, not market narrative. Shipping with placeholder launch docs or vision-heavy copy would have created immediate expectation drift.

statement: Positioned tusq.dev v0.1.0 as an open-source capability compiler CLI for Express, Fastify, and NestJS that delivers scan, manifest, approval, compile, review, and describe-only MCP serve.
rationale: This matches the verified command surface, supported frameworks, and QA-confirmed behavior without importing deferred roadmap claims.

statement: Aligned the four required launch artifacts plus the README and homepage around explicit defendable claims and non-claims.
rationale: Consistent entry-point messaging reduces overclaim risk, especially around live execution, runtime learning, framework coverage, and review UX.

statement: Planning artifacts are complete and internally consistent. Phase transition to implementation requires human approval per the planning_signoff gate.
rationale: All 4 required artifacts (PM_SIGNOFF.md, ROADMAP.md, SYSTEM_SPEC.md, command-surface.md) exist with approved content. PM_SIGNOFF is set to Approved: YES. The scope is well-defined (Docusaurus site with homepage, 7 docs pages, 2 blog posts), out-of-scope items are explicit, and acceptance criteria are testable. No contradictions found between artifacts.

statement: Fixed routing from attempt 1: proposed_next_role must be from allowed set [pm, product_marketing, eng_director, human]. Using 'human' since planning_signoff gate requires human approval.
rationale: Attempt 1 failed because proposed_next_role was 'dev' which is not in the allowed_next_roles for the planning phase. The planning_signoff gate explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to implementation.

statement: Planning artifacts are verified complete post-human-approval. Phase transition to implementation is warranted.
rationale: All 4 required artifacts (PM_SIGNOFF.md, ROADMAP.md, SYSTEM_SPEC.md, command-surface.md) exist, PM_SIGNOFF contains 'Approved: YES', the human escalation was resolved via operator_unblock confirming approval. No changes have been made to the artifacts since the last review — they remain internally consistent with clear scope (Docusaurus docs site in website/), 22 acceptance criteria, 7 milestones, and explicit out-of-scope boundaries.

reasons: .planning/SYSTEM_SPEC.md must define ## Interface before planning can exit.
.planning/command-surface.md: Document must contain sections: ## Primary Commands, ## Flags And Options, ## Failure UX

statement: Added missing gate-required sections to SYSTEM_SPEC.md and command-surface.md to satisfy the planning_signoff structural checks.
rationale: The planning_signoff gate requires SYSTEM_SPEC.md to contain a ## Interface section (system_spec semantics) and command-surface.md to contain ## Primary Commands, ## Flags And Options, ## Failure UX sections (section_check semantics). The prior turn's phase transition request was rejected because these sections were absent. Content is appropriate for the Docusaurus website platform scope — Interface covers build commands, web navigation, and content authoring; command-surface sections cover npm scripts, Docusaurus config options, and build/dev failure modes.
